title: Introduction to the parglm package
text: , perform estimate logistic regression 1000000 observations 50 covariates. vary number cores used nthreads argument parglm.control. method argument sets method used. LINPACK uses pivoted QR (dqrdc2) glm.fit final decomposition; LAPACK uses DGEQP3 LAPACK throughout; FAST solves normal equations Fisher information, similar speedglm package. plot shows median run times versus number cores. Coloured horizontal lines show single-threaded reference times glm, speedglm, glm2, fastglm, bigglm, bam. used glm.fit parglm.fit. make relative difference bigger call e.g., model.matrix model.frame take time. show point, first compute much time takes make plot. black solid line computation time model.matrix model.frame. Plot runtime versus number cores. open circles FAST method filled circles LINPACK method. FAST method, speedglm, fastglm compute Fisher information solve normal equation. advantages terms computation cost may lead unstable solutions. can alter number observations parallel chunk block_size argument parglm.control. single threaded performance parglm may slower coefficients. cause seems difference LAPACK LINPACK implementation. presumably due either QR decomposition method /qr.qty method. Windows, parglm seems slower build Rtools reason seems qr.qty method LAPACK, dormqr, slower LINPACK method, dqrsl. illustration computation times machine.
code: ##### # simulate n # number of observations #> [1] 1000000 p # number of covariates #> [1] 50 set.seed(68024947) X <- matrix(rnorm(n * p, 1/p, 1/sqrt(p)), n, ncol = p) df <- data.frame(y = 1/(1 + exp(-(rowSums(X) - 1))) > runif(n), X) ##### # compute and measure time. Setup call to make library(microbenchmark) library(speedglm) library(fastglm) library(glm2) library(biglm) library(mgcv) library(parglm) X_mat <- model.matrix(y ~ ., df) biglm_form <- reformulate(names(df)[-1], response = "y") bam_form   <- biglm_form cl <- list(   quote(microbenchmark),   glm      = quote(glm     (y ~ ., binomial(), df)),   speedglm = quote(speedglm(y ~ ., family = binomial(), data = df)),   glm2     = quote(glm2    (y ~ ., binomial(), df)),   fastglm  = quote(fastglm (X_mat, as.numeric(df$y), family = binomial())),   bigglm   = quote(bigglm  (biglm_form, df, family = binomial())),   bam      = quote(bam     (bam_form, family = binomial(), data = df)),   times = 11L) tfunc <- function(method = "LINPACK", nthreads)   parglm(y ~ ., binomial(), df, control = parglm.control(method = method,                                                          nthreads = nthreads)) cl <- c(   cl, lapply(1:n_threads, function(i) bquote(tfunc(nthreads = .(i))))) names(cl)[9:(9L + n_threads - 1L)] <- paste0("parglm-LINPACK-", 1:n_threads)  cl <- c(   cl, lapply(1:n_threads, function(i) bquote(tfunc(     nthreads = .(i), method = "FAST")))) names(cl)[(9L + n_threads):(9L + 2L * n_threads - 1L)] <-   paste0("parglm-FAST-", 1:n_threads)  cl <- c(   cl, lapply(1:n_threads, function(i) bquote(tfunc(     nthreads = .(i), method = "LAPACK")))) names(cl)[(9L + 2L * n_threads):(9L + 3L * n_threads - 1L)] <-   paste0("parglm-LAPACK-", 1:n_threads)  cl <- as.call(cl) cl # the call we make #> microbenchmark(glm = glm(y ~ ., binomial(), df), speedglm = speedglm(y ~  #>     ., family = binomial(), data = df), glm2 = glm2(y ~ ., binomial(),  #>     df), fastglm = fastglm(X_mat, as.numeric(df$y), family = binomial()),  #>     bigglm = bigglm(biglm_form, df, family = binomial()), bam = bam(bam_form,  #>         family = binomial(), data = df), times = 11L, `parglm-LINPACK-1` = tfunc(nthreads = 1L),  #>     `parglm-LINPACK-2` = tfunc(nthreads = 2L), `parglm-LINPACK-3` = tfunc(nthreads = 3L),  #>     `parglm-LINPACK-4` = tfunc(nthreads = 4L), `parglm-LINPACK-5` = tfunc(nthreads = 5L),  #>     `parglm-LINPACK-6` = tfunc(nthreads = 6L), `parglm-LINPACK-7` = tfunc(nthreads = 7L),  #>     `parglm-LINPACK-8` = tfunc(nthreads = 8L), `parglm-LINPACK-9` = tfunc(nthreads = 9L),  #>     `parglm-LINPACK-10` = tfunc(nthreads = 10L), `parglm-FAST-1` = tfunc(nthreads = 1L,  #>         method = "FAST"), `parglm-FAST-2` = tfunc(nthreads = 2L,  #>         method = "FAST"), `parglm-FAST-3` = tfunc(nthreads = 3L,  #>         method = "FAST"), `parglm-FAST-4` = tfunc(nthreads = 4L,  #>         method = "FAST"), `parglm-FAST-5` = tfunc(nthreads = 5L,  #>         method = "FAST"), `parglm-FAST-6` = tfunc(nthreads = 6L,  #>         method = "FAST"), `parglm-FAST-7` = tfunc(nthreads = 7L,  #>         method = "FAST"), `parglm-FAST-8` = tfunc(nthreads = 8L,  #>         method = "FAST"), `parglm-FAST-9` = tfunc(nthreads = 9L,  #>         method = "FAST"), `parglm-FAST-10` = tfunc(nthreads = 10L,  #>         method = "FAST"), `parglm-LAPACK-1` = tfunc(nthreads = 1L,  #>         method = "LAPACK"), `parglm-LAPACK-2` = tfunc(nthreads = 2L,  #>         method = "LAPACK"), `parglm-LAPACK-3` = tfunc(nthreads = 3L,  #>         method = "LAPACK"), `parglm-LAPACK-4` = tfunc(nthreads = 4L,  #>         method = "LAPACK"), `parglm-LAPACK-5` = tfunc(nthreads = 5L,  #>         method = "LAPACK"), `parglm-LAPACK-6` = tfunc(nthreads = 6L,  #>         method = "LAPACK"), `parglm-LAPACK-7` = tfunc(nthreads = 7L,  #>         method = "LAPACK"), `parglm-LAPACK-8` = tfunc(nthreads = 8L,  #>         method = "LAPACK"), `parglm-LAPACK-9` = tfunc(nthreads = 9L,  #>         method = "LAPACK"), `parglm-LAPACK-10` = tfunc(nthreads = 10L,  #>         method = "LAPACK"))  out <- eval(cl) s <- summary(out) # result from `microbenchmark` print(s[, c("expr", "min", "mean", "median", "max")], digits  = 3,       row.names = FALSE) #>               expr   min  mean median   max #>                glm  5473  5642   5561  6010 #>           speedglm  4021  4172   4151  4365 #>               glm2  5526  5838   5663  6868 #>            fastglm  1977  2221   2186  2642 #>             bigglm  6585  7076   7061  7663 #>                bam 12198 12637  12669 13209 #>   parglm-LINPACK-1  6718  7079   7094  7397 #>   parglm-LINPACK-2  3878  4155   4130  4864 #>   parglm-LINPACK-3  2836  3053   3016  3303 #>   parglm-LINPACK-4  2446  2585   2559  2786 #>   parglm-LINPACK-5  2138  2251   2219  2538 #>   parglm-LINPACK-6  1954  2177   2222  2553 #>   parglm-LINPACK-7  1866  2082   1976  2446 #>   parglm-LINPACK-8  1825  2000   1992  2193 #>   parglm-LINPACK-9  1906  2050   1969  2382 #>  parglm-LINPACK-10  1842  1954   1920  2186 #>      parglm-FAST-1  3871  4017   4010  4321 #>      parglm-FAST-2  1994  2282   2285  2595 #>      parglm-FAST-3  1573  1780   1681  2479 #>      parglm-FAST-4  1258  1388   1360  1519 #>      parglm-FAST-5  1161  1262   1243  1499 #>      parglm-FAST-6  1080  1180   1182  1344 #>      parglm-FAST-7   996  1129   1027  1577 #>      parglm-FAST-8   909  1072   1001  1592 #>      parglm-FAST-9   883  1014   1021  1128 #>     parglm-FAST-10   926  1092   1049  1478 #>    parglm-LAPACK-1  7041  7245   7174  7585 #>    parglm-LAPACK-2  3940  4079   4048  4428 #>    parglm-LAPACK-3  2898  3074   3007  3543 #>    parglm-LAPACK-4  2454  2601   2602  2811 #>    parglm-LAPACK-5  2109  2290   2226  2700 #>    parglm-LAPACK-6  1973  2169   2096  2887 #>    parglm-LAPACK-7  1882  2135   2089  2476 #>    parglm-LAPACK-8  1810  2019   1986  2545 #>    parglm-LAPACK-9  1828  2016   2019  2265 #>   parglm-LAPACK-10  1808  1940   1909  2120 modmat_time <- microbenchmark(   modmat_time = {     mf <- model.frame(y ~ ., df); model.matrix(terms(mf), mf)   }, times = 10) modmat_time # time taken by `model.matrix` and `model.frame` #> Unit: milliseconds #>         expr       min        lq        mean    median         uq        max neval #>  modmat_time 75.901045 87.949182 115.3905681 92.080219 151.871749 204.918779    10 par(mar = c(4.5, 4.5, .5, 9)) o <- aggregate(time ~ expr, out, median)[, 2] / 10^9 ylim <- range(o, 0); ylim[2] <- ylim[2] + .04 * diff(ylim)  o_linpack <- o[7L:(n_threads + 6L)] o_fast    <- o[(n_threads + 7L):(2L * n_threads + 6L)] o_lapack  <- o[(2L * n_threads + 7L):(3L * n_threads + 6L)]  ref_cols <- c("#E69F00", "#56B4E9", "#009E73", "#0072B2", "#D55E00", "#CC79A7") ref_lty  <- c("dashed", "dotted", "dotdash", "longdash", "twodash", "1373")  plot(1:n_threads, o_linpack, xlab = "Number of cores", yaxs = "i",      ylim = ylim, ylab = "Run time (s)", pch = 16, cex = 1.4) points(1:n_threads, o_fast,   pch = 1, cex = 1.4, lwd = 2) points(1:n_threads, o_lapack, pch = 4, cex = 1.4, lwd = 2) for (i in 1:6)   abline(h = o[i], lty = ref_lty[i], col = ref_cols[i], lwd = 2) abline(h = median(modmat_time$time) / 10^9, lty = "solid", col = "black",        lwd = 2) usr <- par("usr") par(xpd = TRUE) legend(x = usr[2], y = usr[4], xjust = 0, yjust = 1,        legend = c("glm", "speedglm", "glm2", "fastglm", "bigglm", "bam",                   "model.matrix", "parglm LINPACK", "parglm FAST",                   "parglm LAPACK"),        lty = c(ref_lty, "solid", NA, NA, NA),        lwd = c(2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, NA, NA, NA),        col = c(ref_cols, "black", "black", "black", "black"),        pch = c(NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, 16, 1, 4),        bty = "n") qr1 <- qr(X) qr2 <- qr(X, LAPACK = TRUE) microbenchmark::microbenchmark(   `qr LINPACK`     = qr(X),   `qr LAPACK`      = qr(X, LAPACK = TRUE),   `qr.qty LINPACK` = qr.qty(qr1, df$y),   `qr.qty LAPACK`  = qr.qty(qr2, df$y),   times = 11) #> Unit: milliseconds #>            expr         min           lq         mean      median           uq #>      qr LINPACK 1210.613355 1233.6329690 1305.5175198 1295.412958 1371.0355515 #>       qr LAPACK 1375.848316 1389.2720645 1454.8008717 1400.265251 1473.0613045 #>  qr.qty LINPACK   78.596262   80.7090945  208.7720708  133.422364  276.6767945 #>   qr.qty LAPACK  549.984865  552.9238065  584.0375455  558.847712  585.2843685 #>          max neval #>  1461.413676    11 #>  1818.412689    11 #>   533.635295    11 #>   732.217237    11

title: Introduction to the parglm package
text: smaller datasets per-call overhead parglm’s thread pool can exceed gains parallelism. repeat benchmark n = 100,000 n = 10,000, dropping bam since aimed much larger problems. Note default block_size parglm.control 10000, n = 10,000 work split across threads parglm effectively runs single-threaded regardless nthreads.

title: Introduction to the parglm package
text: Plot runtime versus number cores n = 100,000.

title: Introduction to the parglm package
text: Plot runtime versus number cores n = 10,000.

title: Introduction to the parglm package
text: per-row cost method scales roughly \(p^2\) (computing \(X'WX\) IRLS iteration), parglm’s thread-pool overhead roughly fixed per call. two effects compete: small \(p\) overhead dominates parallel benefit, larger \(p\) parallel work amortizes overhead parglm pulls ahead. contrast n = 100,000, p = 5 (overhead wins) n = 1,000,000, p = 20 (parallel work wins).

title: Introduction to the parglm package
text: Plot runtime versus number cores n = 100,000 p = 5.

title: Introduction to the parglm package
text: Plot runtime versus number cores n = 1,000,000 p = 20.

title: Parallel GLM
text: parglm package provides parallel estimation method generalized linear models without compiling multithreaded LAPACK BLAS. can install release version CRAN install development version GitHub calling: See vignette example run times details.

title: parglm: Parallel GLM — parglm-package
text: Provides parallel estimation method generalized linear models without compiling multithreaded LAPACK BLAS.

title: parglm: Parallel GLM — parglm-package
text: Maintainer: Tom Palmer remlapmot@hotmail.com (ORCID) Authors: Tom Palmer remlapmot@hotmail.com (ORCID) Benjamin Christoffersen boennecd@gmail.com (ORCID) contributors: Anthony Williams [copyright holder] Boost developers [copyright holder]

what: Auxiliary for Controlling GLM Fitting in Parallel — parglm.control
title: Auxiliary for Controlling GLM Fitting in Parallel — parglm.control
text: Auxiliary function parglm fitting.

title: Auxiliary for Controlling GLM Fitting in Parallel — parglm.control
code: parglm.control(   epsilon = 1e-08,   maxit = 25,   trace = FALSE,   nthreads = 1L,   block_size = NULL,   method = "LINPACK" )

title: Auxiliary for Controlling GLM Fitting in Parallel — parglm.control
text: epsilon positive convergence tolerance. maxit integer giving maximal number IWLS iterations. trace logical indicating output produced estimation. nthreads number cores use. may get best performance using number physical cores data set sufficiently large. Using number physical CPUs/cores may yield best performance (check number e.g., calling parallel::detectCores(logical = FALSE)). block_size number observation include parallel block. method string specifying method use. Either "LINPACK", "LAPACK", "FAST".

title: Auxiliary for Controlling GLM Fitting in Parallel — parglm.control
text: list components named arguments.

title: Auxiliary for Controlling GLM Fitting in Parallel — parglm.control
text: LINPACK method uses QR method glm.fit final QR decomposition. dqrdc2 method described qr. QR decompositions last made DGEQP3 LAPACK. See Wood, Goude, Shaw (2015) details QR method. FAST method computes Fisher information solves normal equation. faster less numerically stable.

title: Auxiliary for Controlling GLM Fitting in Parallel — parglm.control
text: Wood, S.N., Goude, Y. & Shaw S. (2015) Generalized additive models large datasets. Journal Royal Statistical Society, Series C 64(1): 139-155.

title: Auxiliary for Controlling GLM Fitting in Parallel — parglm.control
code: # use one core f1 <- parglm(mpg ~ wt + hp, data = mtcars, family = Gamma(link = "log"),              control = parglm.control(nthreads = 1L))  # use two cores (mtcars has 32 rows, sufficient for 2 threads) f2 <- parglm(mpg ~ wt + hp, data = mtcars, family = Gamma(link = "log"),              control = parglm.control(nthreads = 2L)) all.equal(coef(f1), coef(f2)) #> [1] TRUE

what: Fitting Generalized Linear Models in Parallel — parglm
title: Fitting Generalized Linear Models in Parallel — parglm
text: Function like glm can make computation parallel. function supports families listed family. See "vignette("parglm", "parglm")" run time examples.

title: Fitting Generalized Linear Models in Parallel — parglm
code: parglm(   formula,   family = gaussian,   data,   weights,   subset,   na.action,   start = NULL,   offset,   control = list(...),   contrasts = NULL,   model = TRUE,   x = FALSE,   y = TRUE,   ... )  parglm.fit(   x,   y,   weights = rep(1, NROW(x)),   start = NULL,   etastart = NULL,   mustart = NULL,   offset = rep(0, NROW(x)),   family = gaussian(),   control = list(),   intercept = TRUE,   ... )

title: Fitting Generalized Linear Models in Parallel — parglm
text: formula object class formula. family family object. data optional data frame, list environment containing variables model. weights optional vector 'prior weights' used fitting process. NULL numeric vector. subset optional vector specifying subset observations used fitting process. na.action function indicates happen data contain NAs. start starting values parameters linear predictor. offset can used specify priori known component included linear predictor fitting. control list parameters controlling fitting process. parglm.fit passed parglm.control. contrasts optional list. See contrasts.arg model.matrix.default. model logical value indicating whether model frame included component returned value. x, y parglm: logical values indicating whether response vector model matrix used fitting process returned components returned value. parglm.fit: x design matrix dimension n * p, y vector observations length n. ... parglm: arguments used form default control argument supplied directly. parglm.fit: unused. etastart starting values linear predictor. supported. mustart starting values vector means. supported. intercept logical. intercept included null model?

title: Fitting Generalized Linear Models in Parallel — parglm
text: glm object returned glm differs mainly qr element. qr element object returned parglm(.fit) \(R\) matrix QR decomposition.

title: Fitting Generalized Linear Models in Parallel — parglm
text: current implementation uses min(.integer(n / p), nthreads) threads n number observations, p number covariates, nthreads nthreads element list returned parglm.control. Thus, likely little () reduction computation time p almost equal n. current implementation handle p > n.

title: Fitting Generalized Linear Models in Parallel — parglm
code: # mtcars has 32 rows, sufficient for 2 threads (>= 16 rows per thread) f1 <- glm   (mpg ~ wt + hp, data = mtcars, family = Gamma(link = "log")) f2 <- parglm(mpg ~ wt + hp, data = mtcars, family = Gamma(link = "log"),              control = parglm.control(nthreads = 2L)) all.equal(coef(f1), coef(f2)) #> [1] TRUE

title: parglm (development version)
text: Add biglm, fastglm, glm2, mgcv timing comparison vignette. add timings fewer observations fewer coefficients. Bump roxygen2 8.0.0

title: parglm 0.1.8
text: CRAN release: 2026-04-21 Remove specification C++11 Amend std::result_of std::invoke_result header file Fix infinite recursion QR_base::qyt removing std::move Require C++17 Linux, macOS, Windows New maintainer - Tom Palmer

title: parglm 0.1.7
text: CRAN release: 2021-10-14 Avoid virtual function calls remove macros. Fix issue due new STRICT_R_HEADERS variable Rcpp.

title: parglm 0.1.6
text: CRAN release: 2020-08-10 Avoid memory allocations. Fix test issue ATLAS.

title: parglm 0.1.4
text: CRAN release: 2020-01-07 stops variables observations. Previously, caused crash. Handle Fortran string length argument.

title: parglm 0.1.3
text: CRAN release: 2019-03-18 Fix bug found Valgrind.

title: parglm 0.1.2
text: CRAN release: 2019-03-14 Minor changes implementation. Fix bugs patched R oldrel R.

title: parglm 0.1.1
text: CRAN release: 2019-01-19 FAST method added computes Fisher information solves normal equation speedglm. One change decreased computation time. Minor bug fixes.
